- When I receive lab results back from CATIE / Panama's IDIAP soil lab (pH, P, K, Ca, Mg, organic matter, CEC, micronutrients), I want the system to turn them into a per-plot, per-variety fertilization & amendment plan with exact product, rate (kg/ha), timing windows, and cost, so I order and apply the right inputs instead of blanket-spreading the same NPK everywhere.
- When I'm building the season's input budget across all 10 plots at 1,350–1,700 masl, I want to compare nutrient demand by variety and yield target against current soil reserves and turn the gap into a costed purchase + application calendar, so the owner can approve spend and I never run out of lime or compost mid-application.
- When an inspector or specialty-green buyer audits us for organic / Rainforest Alliance compliance, I want a complete, dated record of every amendment applied (product, batch, rate, applicator, REI) per plot, so I can prove inputs were approved-list and protect the certification and the price premium.

**Features:**
- **Soil & Tissue Test Lab Records** — A per-plot timeline of lab reports (pH, organic matter %, CEC, N-P-K, Ca/Mg/S, micronutrients B/Zn/Mn/Fe/Cu, base saturation; plus leaf-tissue analysis) with trend lines so deficiencies are caught early and Janette diagnoses the real cause of the Paso Ancho slump instead of guessing.
- **Sampling Plan & Geotagged Sample Collection** — Generates a stratified sampling schedule per plot/block (composite cores, GPS-tagged), prints lab submission labels with the plot id, and tracks chain-of-custody from field to lab, so results map cleanly back to the right hectares and sampling actually happens on a cadence.
- **Fertilization & Amendment Plan Builder** — Turns a soil/tissue result + yield target + variety into a recommended program (lime to correct pH for acidic volcanic Andisols, P/K, organic compost, foliar micros) with rate kg/ha, split timing tied to phenology and rainy season, and a per-plot cost — eliminating blanket over-application and tying nutrition spend to actual need.
- **Input Inventory & Application Log** — Tracks fertilizer/lime/compost stock, links each application to a plot, product batch, applicator (worker), rate, and date (auto-creating a 'Fertilizing'/'Soil' task), so the owner sees true cost-per-plot and there's a defensible, dated record for every kg applied.
- **Compliance & Approved-Input Register** — Flags whether each product is on the organic / Rainforest Alliance approved list, enforces it at plan-build time, and exports an audit-ready amendment dossier per plot — protecting the certification and the specialty-green price premium.
- **Soil Health Scorecard on the Plot** — A composite soil-health index (pH-in-range, OM%, CEC, base-saturation balance, erosion/cover-crop status) surfaced on the existing plot card / status, so 'healthy / watch / at-risk' is backed by soil data, not just a manual lastInspected date.

**Integrations / vendors / hardware:** IDIAP (Instituto de Investigación Agropecuaria de Panamá) soil & foliar lab — the national Panama lab; report ingestion via PDF/CSV upload + parse, CATIE (Costa Rica) and Agrilab / Brookside Laboratories — regional/international soil & tissue labs for buyers wanting independent assays; standardized result import (PDF/CSV, and LIMS API where available), Cropster — push per-lot agronomy/soil context so green-coffee buyers see the terroir/nutrition story behind a lot, SoilGrids / ISRIC and FAO Harmonized World Soil Database — baseline soil property priors for Volcán's volcanic Andisols to seed plots before first lab test, Sentinel-2 / Planet NDVI & chlorophyll (red-edge) indices via Sentinel Hub or EOS Crop Monitoring — remote vigor signal to flag plots that warrant a soil/tissue test, Open-Meteo / OpenWeather rainfall API — schedule amendment applications around the rainy season and avoid leaching/wash-off (the app already has a weather module to extend), Yara / ICL / regional Panama agro-input distributors (e.g. Agroservicios) price feeds or catalog CSV — cost the fertilization plan against real local product prices, 4R Nutrient Stewardship and IFA fertilizer-recommendation frameworks; FAO/IPNI nutrient-removal coefficients for coffee — the recommendation logic standard, Rainforest Alliance & USDA Organic / NOP approved-input lists — compliance enforcement at plan-build time, Optional field hardware: soil pH/EC/moisture probes (e.g. METER TEROS / Sentek) for cheap inter-test monitoring; QR/barcode label printer for sample chain-of-custody

**Data model additions:** soil_tests (id, plot_id FK, sample_date, lab_name, depth_cm, ph, organic_matter_pct, cec, base_saturation_pct, n_pct, p_ppm, k_cmol, ca_cmol, mg_cmol, s_ppm, micronutrients jsonb {b,zn,mn,fe,cu}, texture, report_url), tissue_tests (id, plot_id FK, sample_date, lab_name, leaf_position, n,p,k,ca,mg,s,b,zn,mn,fe,cu values, report_url) — leaf analysis complements soil, sampling_events (id, plot_id FK, planned_date, collected_date, num_cores, gps_points jsonb, collected_by worker_id FK, status, chain_of_custody_code), fertilization_plans (id, plot_id FK, season, variety, yield_target_kg, status [draft/approved/active/done], total_cost_usd, approved_by, basis_soil_test_id FK), fertilization_plan_items (id, plan_id FK, product_id FK, rate_kg_ha, total_kg, application_window, phenology_stage, split_number, cost_usd), amendment_products (id, name, type [lime/compost/synthetic-npk/foliar/gypsum], analysis {n,p,k,...}, unit_cost_usd, supplier, organic_approved bool, ra_approved bool, rei_hours), amendment_applications (id, plot_id FK, plan_item_id FK nullable, product_id FK, batch_code, applied_date, rate_kg_ha, total_kg, applicator worker_id FK, weather_at_application, task_id FK) — the compliance record, input_inventory (id, product_id FK, on_hand_kg, location, reorder_point_kg, last_received_date), soil_health_scores (plot_id FK, as_of_date, composite_score, ph_status, om_status, cec_status, balance_status, drivers jsonb) — feeds the plot status

**AI / automation:**
- LLM-powered lab-report parser: drop in a IDIAP/CATIE/Brookside soil PDF and it extracts pH/OM/CEC/nutrient values into structured soil_tests rows, normalizing the wildly different lab report layouts
- Recommendation engine: given soil + tissue results, variety, yield target and Volcán Andisol priors, generate a 4R-aligned fertilization & liming plan (rate, timing, split, product) with a plain-language rationale Janette can edit and approve
- Deficiency diagnosis assistant: correlate NDVI/red-edge dips, yield drops, and visible-symptom photos against soil/tissue values to name the most likely limiting nutrient and recommend a confirming test (great for Paso Ancho's at-risk status)
- Rainfall-aware application scheduler: auto-suggest amendment dates that avoid heavy-rain leaching and respect REI, auto-creating the Fertilizing/Soil tasks on the existing board
- Anomaly + reorder alerts: flag a plot whose soil pH is drifting below the coffee optimum (5.0–5.5) or whose nutrient buffer crosses the reorder point, and draft the purchase order
- Compliance guard: at plan-build time, an AI check blocks any non-approved product for an organic/RA-certified plot and explains the substitution

**World-class:** "World-class soil management closes a continuous loop: scheduled stratified soil + leaf-tissue sampling on every plot, results auto-parsed and trended, turned into a 4R-aligned, variety-specific, rainfall-timed and costed fertilization/liming plan that the owner approves with one tap; every kg applied is logged against a plot, product batch, and applicator with full organic/Rainforest Alliance compliance and audit export; remote NDVI/red-edge and cheap in-field probes catch problems between tests; and the soil-health index visibly drives the plot's status and is provable to buyers. It's site-specific (per-hectare, not blanket), proactive (catches deficiency before yield drops), and turns Volcán's volcanic-soil terroir into a documented quality and pricing story — not a binder of paper lab reports nobody re-reads."

**Coffee-specific angle:** "Volcán sits on volcanic Andisols that are naturally acidic and can fix phosphorus and tie up micronutrients — pH and P availability management is THE soil lever for coffee here, and the optimal coffee pH band (5.0–5.5) plus high-altitude (1,350–1,700 masl) shade-grown context must drive the recommendation logic. Janson grows premium Geisha at the highest, healthiest plots (Tizingal Alto 1,690m, Las Lagunas 1,700m at 55–60% shade) where over-fertilizing can flatten the delicate floral cup; nutrition has to be tuned per varietal (Geisha vs vigorous Caturra/Catuaí vs large-seeded Pacamara) because each has different nutrient removal and quality sensitivity. Because Janson sells specialty green and runs a 200-ha reserve in quetzal habitat, organic/Rainforest-Alliance-compatible amendments (compost from their own pulp, controlled liming, shade-litter cycling) and watershed-safe timing aren't just compliance — they're the brand and the premium. Soil data per plot becomes part of the lot's terroir story that buyers on Cropster pay up for."


#### Agronomy advisory & crop planning — varietals, density, shade, renovation
*Priority:* **differentiator**  ·  *Opportunity:* **9/10**

**Persona:** Janette, the farm agronomist (and the owner-manager who signs off on multi-year capital plans). The agronomist owns plot-by-plot health, varietal/shade decisions and the renovation calendar; the owner cares about the 10-15 year productivity-and-quality trajectory of each block on Volcan Baru's slopes. Secondary users: block supervisors who execute the resulting prescriptions, and the nursery lead who must produce the right seedlings 12-18 months ahead.

**Jobs-to-be-done:**
- When a Geisha block like Paso Ancho slips to 'at-risk' and yield-per-tree is sliding (4,100 of 12,600 kg this season), I want a data-backed verdict on whether to renovate, stump-rehabilitate, or replant with a different varietal, so I can stop pouring inputs into trees past their productive peak and schedule the work before the next dry season.
- When I'm deciding what to put on a newly cleared parcel at 1,690 masl, I want a varietal + density + shade recommendation grounded in that parcel's altitude, soil test, slope/aspect and local disease pressure, so I can match World Coffee Research variety guidance to our microclimate and maximize cup-score-per-hectare instead of copying the neighboring block.
- When I walk the canopy and the guabo (Inga) shade is overgrown, casting >60% on a block that wants ~45%, I want a shade-management prescription tied to satellite canopy data and the bloom calendar, so I can thin the right trees at the right time to balance bean maturation, leaf-rust suppression and nitrogen fixation.
- When I plan the nursery 12-18 months out, I want the renovation schedule to auto-generate a seedling demand forecast by varietal, so I can germinate exactly the right number of Geisha, Pacamara and Caturra plants and never stall a replant for lack of stock.

**Features:**
- **Plot Agronomy Profile (deep plot record)** — Turns today's flat Plot into a living agronomic record: soil-test history (pH, N-P-K, organic matter, Ca/Mg, base saturation), slope/aspect, rootstock, planting density vs. target, tree age cohort, and a rolling health timeline. Gives every decision a single source of truth instead of tribal memory.
- **Renovation & Rehabilitation Planner** — Per-block decision engine that flags trees past their productive peak (age + yield-decline + cup-trend) and recommends renovate vs. stump (recepa) vs. interplant vs. hold, with a phased multi-year calendar so no more than a safe fraction of bearing area is ever out of production at once. Protects cash flow while resetting the productivity curve.
- **Varietal & Density Advisor** — Given a parcel's altitude, soil, aspect and disease pressure, recommends a varietal mix, planting density (trees/ha) and row/contour layout, scored on expected yield, cup potential, leaf-rust resistance and price realization. Matches Janson's microclimates to the right cultivar instead of one-size-fits-all.
- **Shade & Agroforestry Manager** — Tracks shade species (Inga/guabo, Erythrina), canopy % per block from satellite + ground truth, and issues thinning/pollarding prescriptions timed to the bloom and rust calendar, with nitrogen-fixation credits feeding the fertilization plan. Optimizes the shade-yield-quality trade-off that defines high-altitude Panama coffee.
- **Crop-Year Planting Calendar & Phenology Tracker** — Models the flowering-to-harvest cycle per block (Geisha runs up to ~10 months on Baru), tracks bloom events against rainfall, and sequences agronomy tasks (fertilize, prune, foliar, shade-thin) to the phenological stage. Right action, right week, every block.
- **Nursery & Seedling Demand Forecast** — Reads the renovation/replant calendar and back-calculates seedling germination orders by varietal 12-18 months ahead, tracking nursery inventory and survival rates. No replant ever stalls for lack of plants; no over-germination waste.
- **Block Economics & Renovation ROI** — Couples each agronomy prescription to a cost/yield/quality projection so the owner sees payback years and lost-production cost before approving a renovation. Capital decisions become numbers, not hunches.

**Integrations / vendors / hardware:** World Coffee Research Variety Catalog (cultivar agronomic + disease-resistance reference data) and WCR Verified seed-lot sourcing, Sentinel-2 imagery via Sentinel Hub / Copernicus Data Space (free 10m NDVI, canopy + shade-fraction time series), Planet Labs PlanetScope (near-daily 3m imagery for early stress/canopy-gap detection) as a paid upgrade, Cropster (cup-score / quality data per lot, to close the variety -> cup-quality feedback loop), Soil testing labs: regional Panama lab plus IDIAP (Instituto de Investigacion Agropecuaria de Panama); ingest lab PDFs/CSVs of pH, NPK, OM, CEC, Weather/phenology APIs: Open-Meteo (free) and a local Volcan station for growing-degree-days, bloom and rust risk modeling, PROMECAFE / CATIE and CENICAFE agronomic models for leaf-rust (roya) and broca pressure forecasting in Central America, Certification bodies for agronomy evidence: Rainforest Alliance, organic (USDA NOP / EU), and Bird Friendly (Smithsonian) shade-cover standards, Soil/canopy sensor hardware: SoilOptix or in-field probes, plus drone NDVI (DJI Mavic/Phantom multispectral) for sub-satellite-resolution scouting

**Data model additions:** plot_agronomy_profile (1:1 extension of Plot: rootstock, target_density, slope_deg, aspect, drainage_class, last_renovation_year, productive_peak_year), soil_test (plotId, sampledAt, lab, pH, N, P, K, Ca, Mg, organic_matter_pct, CEC, recommendations), shade_record (plotId, observedAt, canopy_pct, source: satellite|ground|drone, species mix, ndvi), shade_tree_species (name, genus e.g. Inga/Erythrina, n_fixing_bool, target_canopy_pct), renovation_plan (plotId, action: renovate|stump|interplant|hold, scheduled_season, fraction_of_block, status, projected_cost, projected_yield_recovery), varietal_recommendation (parcelId, variety, density, shade_target, rationale, expected_yield, expected_cup_score, rust_resistance), phenology_event (plotId, type: bloom|pin|cherry-set|maturation, date, rainfall_trigger), variety_catalog (variety, optimal_altitude_range, rust_resistance, yield_class, cup_potential, density_guidance) — WCR-sourced reference, nursery_lot (variety, sown_date, qty, survival_pct, ready_date, allocated_to_plotId), tree_cohort (plotId, planted_year, variety, tree_count, current_stage: young|peak|declining)

**AI / automation:**
- Renovation timing model: fuse tree-age cohort, multi-year yield-decline curve and cup-score trend to recommend renovate vs. stump vs. hold per block, with confidence and ROI
- Varietal-fit recommender: rank cultivars for a given parcel from altitude + soil + aspect + disease pressure against the WCR catalog, optimizing for cup-score-per-hectare and rust resistance
- Satellite NDVI -> shade-fraction + stress segmentation: auto-derive canopy % and flag declining zones per block from Sentinel-2/Planet time series, no manual canopy counts
- Phenology/bloom prediction from weather (GDD + rainfall) to auto-schedule fertilization, pruning and shade-thinning windows per block
- Leaf-rust (roya) and broca outbreak forecasting from humidity/temperature + neighboring-region signals, pushing pre-emptive scouting tasks
- Natural-language agronomy copilot: 'why is Paso Ancho at-risk and what do I do this month?' -> grounded answer citing the block's soil, canopy and yield data with a recommended task list
- Seedling-demand auto-forecast: derive nursery germination orders by varietal directly from the approved renovation calendar with survival-rate buffers

**World-class:** World-class is a closed agronomic loop where every block has a digital twin: its soil chemistry, canopy %, tree-age cohorts, phenology and 10-year yield/cup trajectory are continuously fused from satellite NDVI, weather, soil labs and harvest+cup data. The system proactively says 'Paso Ancho's Pacamara is 9 years past peak and rust-pressured; stump 30% this dry season, interplant rust-resistant material, thin guabo to 45% before the April bloom — here's the seedling order, the labor plan, the 4-year ROI, and the cup-score you should expect.' It is prescriptive not descriptive, multi-year-aware so renovation never craters cash flow, microclimate-specific down to the parcel, and it ties varietal/shade choices directly to the cup quality and price that actually pays Janson's bills — the trade-off Hacienda La Esmeralda-tier estates manage by instinct, made explicit and repeatable.

**Coffee-specific angle:** Janson sits at 1,350-1,700 masl on Volcan Baru in Volcan/Chiriqui — the exact terroir that makes Panama Geisha the most prized (and lowest-yielding) coffee in the world. That premium is precisely why prescriptive agronomy matters more here than on a commodity farm: a Geisha tree yields a fraction of a Caturra's, takes up to ~10 months from flower to cherry on Baru, and its market price (often 10-50x commodity) is set by cup score, which the variety + altitude + shade trade-off directly drives. So this pillar must reason in Janson's real cultivars (Geisha, Caturra, Catuai, Pacamara, Typica) and elevation bands — pushing Geisha into the highest, mistiest parcels (Tizingal Alto, Las Lagunas at 1,690-1,700m), reserving robust Caturra/Catuai for the lower warmer blocks, and managing guabo (Inga) shade the way Boquete's trophy estates do. The 200-hectare quetzal reserve also makes shade/agroforestry a conservation asset, opening Bird Friendly (Smithsonian) and Rainforest Alliance certification angles that turn good agronomy into a marketable, premium-justifying story.


#### Phenology & flowering→cherry tracking — bloom prediction, GDD models
*Priority:* **differentiator**  ·  *Opportunity:* **9/10**

**Persona:** Primary: the farm agronomist / production manager (in Janson's case, the family member or hired agrónomo who walks the plots, calls the flowering, and decides when to send crews to which lot). Secondary stakeholders who consume its output: the harvest/labor planner (crew scheduling), the mill manager (forecasting cherry intake for wet-mill capacity), and the owner/commercial lead (committing Geisha lot volumes and timing to buyers/auctions months ahead).

**Jobs-to-be-done:**
- When the first heavy rains break the dry season and I see white blossoms across Tizingal Alto, I want to log that flowering event per plot and immediately get a predicted ripe-cherry window, so I can tell the crew leads and the mill roughly which weeks each lot will peak — instead of guessing from last year's calendar.
- When I'm planning the season 4-6 months out, I want a bloom-to-harvest forecast per plot driven by accumulated growing-degree-days and our actual Volcán weather, so I can stagger pruning, fertilizer, and picking crews across plots that flower and ripen at different times (1,360 m Caturra vs 1,700 m Geisha) and never have two lots peak the same week and overwhelm the wet mill.
- When a buyer asks me in February whether my Las Lagunas Geisha will be ready in time for the Best of Panama auction submission, I want to give a confidence-banded ripening date backed by GDD accumulation to-date, so I can commit volume and timing credibly instead of over-promising.
- When an unseasonal dry spell or cold snap hits between flowering and maturation, I want the system to recompute the GDD trajectory and flag plots whose ripening will slip or whose flowering may misfire, so I can re-stage labor and irrigation before it costs me a uniform harvest.

**Features:**
- **Flowering event log (per plot, per blossom)** — Captures each major blossom date per plot with intensity (% of trees flowering) and a photo, establishing the anchor date (T0) that every maturation prediction counts forward from. Arabica often has multiple staggered blooms per plot; logging each separately is what makes the picking-pass forecast accurate instead of a single blurred average.
- **GDD accumulator + bloom-to-cherry maturation engine** — Pulls daily min/max temperature for each plot's location/altitude and accumulates growing-degree-days from a configurable base (10.5C for Arabica), then projects the ripe-cherry window per cultivar from research-calibrated thermal targets (~1,580-3,090 GDD; ~123-249 days flowering-to-maturity). Turns 'sometime in the dry season' into a plot-level, cultivar-specific ripening date with a confidence band.
- **Blossom-trigger predictor (dry-stress + first-rain alert)** — Tracks the dry-period water deficit and watches the forecast for the first blossom-triggering rain (≥ threshold mm after sustained stress) that breaks flower-bud dormancy, then alerts the agronomist that flowering is imminent across affected plots — so crews are ready to observe and log the bloom the day it happens, the single most important date in the season.
- **BBCH phenology stage tracker** — Walks each plot through the standardized BBCH coffee stages (inflorescence 51-59 -> flowering 60-69 -> berry development 70-79 -> ripening 80-89) with the current stage, GDD-predicted next-stage date, and a per-stage agronomic checklist (e.g. fertilizer timed to pin-head/expansion, the sucrose-loading window at the yellow-green->red transition). Gives the whole farm one shared, scientific vocabulary for where every lot is in its cycle.
- **Farm-wide ripening calendar / Gantt** — A season timeline showing every plot's predicted flowering and ripening windows side by side, with a wet-mill-capacity overlay that flags weeks where too many lots peak at once. The harvest planner and mill manager use it to stagger picking passes and pre-book labor and drying-bed space, smoothing cherry intake instead of firefighting bottlenecks.
- **Picking-pass & ripeness-readiness forecast** — Per plot, predicts how many selective picking passes the lot will need and the optimal start date for each, by combining the maturation curve with the bloom-staggering pattern. Closes the loop with the existing harvests table (ripeness_pct, brix_avg) so predictions are continuously re-tuned against what crews actually pick.

**Integrations / vendors / hardware:** Open-Meteo Historical Weather API (ERA5/ERA5-Land, free, no API key) for daily min/max temperature and precipitation per plot back to 1940 — the data backbone for GDD accumulation and the dry-stress baseline; pairs with Open-Meteo Forecast API for the 16-day forward window, Open-Meteo / OpenWeather forecast feed to drive the first-rain blossom-trigger alert (replacing the current hard-coded presentational weather table), Sentinel-2 imagery via Sentinel Hub or Google Earth Engine for NDVI/EVI time-series per plot polygon — a remote-sensing cross-check that confirms a flowering whitening / canopy greening event at scale across the 200-ha reserve without walking every block, BBCH extended scale for coffee (Arcila-Pulgarin et al., 2002) as the controlled-vocabulary standard for phenological stages — the same codes Cropster and research stations use, so Janson's data is portable and credible to buyers and certifiers, Cropster Origin (export/CSV interop) so the lot-level phenology and predicted-vs-actual harvest timing flow into the cupping/QC and sales records the specialty trade already runs on, On-farm hardware path (future): a Davis Vantage Pro2 or low-cost Ecowitt/Tempest weather station per altitude band, plus optional soil-moisture probes, to localize GDD and the dry-stress index below the ~9-25 km ERA5 grid resolution — critical given Janson spans 1,360-1,700 m on the flank of Barú, Best of Panama / SCA cupping-calendar dates as fixed targets the ripening forecast counts back from for the flagship Geisha lots

**Data model additions:** flowering_events (id, plot_id FK, bloom_date, intensity_pct, blossom_number, triggered_by_rain bool, photo_url, notes) — the T0 anchor for every prediction; multiple rows per plot per season, phenology_observations (id, plot_id FK, observed_date, bbch_stage, stage_label, observer_worker_id FK, photo_url, brix_sample, notes) — ground-truth stage walks that calibrate the model, gdd_daily (plot_id FK, date, t_min, t_max, base_temp, gdd_increment, gdd_cumulative, precip_mm, source) — the accumulated thermal-time series per plot, populated from the weather API, maturation_forecasts (id, plot_id FK, flowering_event_id FK, cultivar, predicted_ripe_start, predicted_ripe_end, confidence_pct, gdd_target, gdd_to_date, model_version, computed_at) — the headline bloom->cherry prediction, recomputed as GDD accrues, phenology_models (id, cultivar, base_temp_c, gdd_to_flowering, gdd_to_maturity, source_citation) — the per-cultivar calibration constants (Geisha/Caturra/Catuai/Pacamara), seeded from literature and tuned against Janson's own predicted-vs-actual history, dry_stress_index (plot_id FK, date, water_deficit_mm, days_in_deficit, trigger_armed bool) — drives the blossom-trigger alert, plot extension: add lat/lon (or boundary polygon GeoJSON) so each plot has a real location for weather pull and Sentinel-2 NDVI clipping — the current schema has altitude but no coordinates

**AI / automation:**
- Bloom-detection from imagery: a vision model run over plot photos and Sentinel-2 NDVI dips/spikes to auto-flag a likely flowering event and pre-fill the log, so a missed observation never silently breaks the season's prediction
- Self-calibrating GDD targets: a regression that learns each cultivar's true thermal-time-to-maturity from Janson's own predicted-date vs actual-harvest-date history (using the existing harvests.ripeness_pct and brix_avg as the maturity signal), tightening confidence bands season over season
- Anomaly + slip alerts: detect when the GDD trajectory diverges from plan (cold snap, heat, drought) and auto-notify the agronomist that specific plots will ripen early/late, with the recomputed window
- Natural-language season briefing: a generated weekly 'state of the bloom' summary per block ('Las Lagunas Geisha at BBCH 75, on track for first pass ~week of Mar 9, 82% confidence; Paso Ancho slipping 8 days after the dry spell') for the owner and crew leads
- Picking-labor optimizer: from the staggered ripening windows, suggest crew sizes and pass dates per plot to keep the wet mill near capacity without overflow

**World-class:** World-class is a farm that knows the harvest date of every plot months before a single cherry turns red, with a quantified confidence band — and re-forecasts automatically as the weather actually unfolds. It means flowering is anticipated (the first-rain trigger alert fires before the bloom, not logged after), tracked in the same BBCH vocabulary research stations and Cropster use, and fused from three independent signals (on-the-ground stage walks, accumulated GDD from localized weather, and satellite NDVI) so no single missed observation breaks the prediction. The model is not a static textbook curve — it learns Janson's own micro-terroir, getting measurably more accurate each season against actual harvest records. The output is operational, not academic: it directly stages labor, wet-mill capacity, and drying beds, and it lets the commercial team commit flagship Geisha volumes and timing to buyers and the Best of Panama calendar with credibility. The benchmark is zero surprise harvests and zero mill bottlenecks caused by two lots peaking the same week.

**Coffee-specific angle:** This is where Janson's specific reality makes phenology a differentiator rather than a nicety. The farm spans 1,360-1,700 masl on the flank of Volcán Barú with four cultivars that flower and ripen on genuinely different clocks — high-altitude Geisha at Las Lagunas (1,700 m) and Tizingal Alto matures weeks later than Caturra at Cuesta de Piedra (1,360 m). That altitude/cultivar spread is the entire reason a per-plot GDD model beats one farm calendar: it turns a logistics headache (everything peaking at once) into a managed, staggered pipeline. Geisha is the crown jewel — late-maturing, terroir-defining, and worth multiples per pound at the Best of Panama auction — so a credible, confidence-banded ripening date for each Geisha lot is directly commercial: it governs when those micro-lots are picked at peak sucrose (the yellow-green->red BBCH 81->88 window the research pinpoints), processed by the right method, and committed to buyers. Volcan's classic green-season pattern — a dry stress period followed by heavy blossom-triggering rains rolling off the volcano — is exactly the climate signal the trigger-predictor is built to read. And it cleanly extends the existing model: flowering_events become the new T0 anchor that the current harvests and processing lot codes count forward from, closing the loop from blossom to cherry to lot to cup.


#### Yield estimation & forecasting (ML)
*Priority:* **differentiator**  ·  *Opportunity:* **9/10**

**Persona:** Primary: the farm agronomist / production manager (at Janson, this is the family member or lead agrónomo who owns the season plan). Secondary consumers: the owner/commercial lead (who pre-sells green coffee to Sucafina-style importers and roasters and must commit volumes by variety months ahead) and the harvest/labor supervisor (who must staff pickers to a credible daily-cherry curve).

**Jobs-to-be-done:**
- When the dry season ends and the first flowering rains hit (the cue that sets this season's crop), I want a defensible per-plot and whole-farm yield forecast in green-kg by variety, so I can pre-sell volumes to importers/roasters and plan financing without over- or under-committing.
- When I'm mid-harvest and only partway through the picking window, I want the forecast to re-estimate the remaining crop from realized daily cherry deliveries plus the standing-crop signal, so I can tell my buyers a tightening number and re-staff pickers before the ripe cherries fall.
- When I look at a single plot like Tizingal Alto (Geisha), I want to see WHY the model expects what it does — flowering rainfall, growing-degree-days, NDVI/NDRE vigor, tree age, biennial on/off phase, last year's actual — so I can trust the number and catch when a plot is silently underperforming (e.g. Paso Ancho at-risk).
- When I plan two to three years out (renovation, new Geisha plantings, financing), I want a multi-season projection that accounts for tree maturity curves and the biennial bearing cycle, so I can decide which blocks to stump/renovate without gutting a peak-bearing year.

**Features:**
- **Per-plot ML yield model (cherry-kg and green-kg)** — Replaces the static hand-entered expectedYieldKg with a trained, versioned model per plot that outputs a point forecast plus a P10/P50/P90 prediction interval — the agronomist gets a number they can defend to a buyer, not a guess.
- **Flowering-event detector & phenology timeline** — Ingests rainfall + satellite NDVI/EVI anomaly to auto-detect each flowering event and project the harvest window (flowering + growing-degree-days to ripeness), so the farm knows when each lot peaks and can sequence picking and the wet mill instead of being surprised.
- **In-season nowcast that re-forecasts from realized harvests** — Blends the pre-season model with cumulative cherriesKg already delivered (a state-space / Kalman-style update) to shrink the remaining-crop uncertainty every day — turns the existing daily harvest log into a live 'crop remaining' and 'days-to-finish' number for staffing and buyer updates.
- **Cherry-to-green conversion & quality-grade yield split** — Forecasts final SELLABLE green-kg by process method and grade (not just cherry) using per-lot cherry:parchment:green ratios, defect/screen-size loss, and Brix/ripeness — so commercial commitments are in the unit buyers actually pay for, with Geisha/specialty split out from commercial grade.
- **Biennial-bearing & tree-maturity engine** — Tracks each plot's on-year/off-year phase and tree-age yield curve so the multi-year projection doesn't naively extrapolate a peak year — protects renovation and financing decisions.
- **Forecast accuracy ledger & model leaderboard** — Logs every forecast vintage against eventual actuals (MAPE/bias per plot, per variety) and benchmarks the ML model against a naive last-year baseline — builds trust over seasons and tells the farm exactly how much to trust each plot's number.
- **Manual standing-crop count workflow (ground truth)** — Mobile-friendly node/branch fruit-count sampling per plot that feeds the model as a calibration input and a fallback when satellite is cloud-occluded — captures the agronomist's eyes-on estimate as structured data, the way pros actually estimate coffee.
- **Scenario & weather-shock simulator** — Lets the agronomist run 'what if the rains come 3 weeks late' / 'a frost or drought' / 'CBB pressure rises' and see the yield and revenue impact, so contracts and labor plans carry an explicit downside case.

**Integrations / vendors / hardware:** Sentinel-2 L2A imagery via Copernicus Data Space Ecosystem / Sentinel Hub (free tier) and Microsoft Planetary Computer for NDVI, NDRE, GNDVI, SAVI, EVI per plot polygon, PlanetScope (Planet Labs) high-cadence 3 m imagery via the NICFI Basemaps program (free for tropical-forest regions incl. Panama) for sub-plot vigor and flowering detection, Open-Meteo and NASA POWER (both free) for daily rainfall, temperature, solar radiation and growing-degree-day accumulation; CHIRPS rainfall for historical baselines, Google Earth Engine (free for research/noncommercial) as the geospatial compute layer to batch zonal NDVI stats over plot polygons, Cropster / Cropster Origin and Sucafina IS (importer systems) export of green-kg and lot quality so forecasts reconcile against the commercial system of record, SCAP (Specialty Coffee Association of Panama) / Best of Panama lot and cupping data to tie quality-grade yield to realized auction/sale value, ICO/Q-grade and SCA green-grading standards (screen size, defect count) as the schema for the quality-grade yield split, Optional on-farm hardware: Davis/Ecowitt weather station + soil-moisture probes (Sentek/METER) and a phone-based fruit-count capture app for ground-truth sampling, scikit-learn / XGBoost / LightGBM (gradient-boosted trees, the proven approach in the coffee-yield literature) trained offline; served as a lightweight inference function on a free serverless tier (Vercel/Supabase Edge)

**Data model additions:** forecast_models — one row per trained model version (plot or variety scope, algorithm, feature set, training window, validation MAPE/bias, created_at) for reproducibility and the leaderboard, yield_forecasts — a forecast vintage: model_id, plot_id, target_season, as_of_date, p10/p50/p90 cherry_kg and green_kg, by variety+grade; many vintages per plot as the season tightens, phenology_events — detected/observed flowering & key stages per plot (event_date, type flowering/pinhead/expansion/ripening, trigger_rainfall_mm, source satellite|rainfall|manual, projected_harvest_window), satellite_observations — per-plot, per-date zonal index stats (ndvi/ndre/gndvi/savi/evi mean+stddev, cloud_pct, scene_id, sensor), weather_daily — per-plot/station daily rain_mm, tmin, tmax, solar, cumulative GDD and water-deficit index (feeds the model and the simulator), standing_crop_samples — manual fruit/node-count samples (plot_id, sample_date, nodes_counted, fruits_per_node, sampler worker_id, extrapolated_cherry_kg), plot_geometry — the plot polygon (GeoJSON) needed to pull satellite/weather per plot; today plots have no spatial footprint, cherry_to_green_ratios — per-lot/method realized cherry:parchment:green factors and grade/defect loss, learned from processing_batches → green output, forecast_accuracy — closes the loop: forecast vintage vs eventual actual harvested_kg, error metrics per plot/variety/season, biennial_state & tree_cohorts — each plot's on/off bearing phase and tree-age cohort yield curve

**AI / automation:**
- Gradient-boosted-tree (XGBoost/LightGBM) ensemble for the core per-plot yield regressor, the approach repeatedly validated in the coffee-yield literature, with SHAP feature attributions surfaced as the 'why' panel so the forecast is explainable rather than a black box
- Phenology-stage CNN/classifier on Sentinel-2 time series to auto-detect flowering (literature reports 93-95% balanced accuracy) and project harvest windows without anyone watching the trees
- State-space / Kalman in-season nowcast that fuses the prior model with realized daily cherriesKg to continuously shrink remaining-crop uncertainty
- Anomaly detection that flags a plot whose NDVI/vigor trajectory diverges from its own history or its variety cohort (early warning for Paso-Ancho-style at-risk lots) and auto-opens an inspection task
- An LLM (Claude) 'forecast narrator' that turns the numbers + SHAP drivers into a plain-Spanish/English brief for the owner and a buyer-ready volume commitment summary, and answers 'why did the Geisha number drop this week?'
- Active-learning prompts that tell the agronomist exactly which plots to ground-sample next to most reduce forecast uncertainty (value-of-information targeting), and seasonal auto-retrain with drift monitoring against the accuracy ledger

**World-class:** A buyer-grade, explainable forecast: every plot shows a P10/P50/P90 green-kg curve by variety and grade that updates daily as flowering rainfall, satellite vigor, and realized harvests arrive — and every number is traceable to its drivers (flowering date, GDD, NDVI anomaly, biennial phase, last-year actual, tree age). World-class means the model beats a naive last-year baseline by a measured margin (sub-10% season-end MAPE at whole-farm scale), surfaces underperformers early enough to act, expresses honest uncertainty instead of false precision, degrades gracefully when satellite is cloud-occluded by leaning on ground counts, and feeds the commercial side a green-kg-by-grade number they can sign a contract against. It quietly retrains each season and shows the farm its own track record so trust is earned, not asserted.

**Coffee-specific angle:** Janson grows Geisha at 1,640-1,700 masl alongside Caturra/Catuaí/Pacamara/Typica lower down — and Geisha yields roughly HALF the cherry-per-tree of Catuaí while selling for 10-100x the price (Panama Geisha has cleared $800+/lb at auction). That asymmetry makes the forecast disproportionately valuable on exactly the lots that are hardest to predict: a few hundred kg of mis-forecast Geisha is worth more than tons of commercial Caturra, and Geisha's thin canopy/shallow roots make it more climate-sensitive, so it needs its own variety- and elevation-conditioned model rather than a farm-average one. The forecast must output green-kg by variety AND grade because Janson pre-sells specialty Geisha lots (Best-of-Panama / direct-to-roaster) on committed volumes — and the 200-ha quetzal reserve's shade-canopy gradient (shadePct already in the model, 33-60%) is itself a strong yield/quality covariate the engine should exploit. Panama's single pronounced dry-season-break flowering also makes the rainfall-triggered flowering detector unusually clean to model here.


#### Per-tree census & lifecycle — planting, pruning/stumping cycles, gaps
*Priority:* **differentiator**  ·  *Opportunity:* **8/10**

**Persona:** Primary: Don Ramiro, the head agronomist / finca manager at Janson — the person responsible for which trees produce, which get pruned, and which get pulled and replanted. Secondary daily users: field supervisors/crew leads doing block walks with a phone (the ones who actually scan/tag trees), the owner-operator (Janson family) for capital-planning decisions on renovation, and the nursery lead who tracks seedlings from germination to field transplant.

**Jobs-to-be-done:**
- When I walk a Geisha block and see gaps, scraggly survivors, and overgrown trees mixed together, I want to know each tree's age, last pruning, and recent yield without trusting my memory or a notebook, so I can decide tree-by-tree what to prune, stump, or pull.
- When a whole block's yield is sliding and I suspect the trees are simply aged out, I want an objective view of the age/pruning-cycle distribution and the productive-life curve, so I can schedule cyclic (rock-and-roll) stumping rows instead of crashing the block's volume all at once.
- When I replant gaps or renovate a section, I want every new seedling tracked from the nursery through transplant with its exact GPS spot and variety, so I can prove field stand counts, forecast when those trees come into production (year 3+), and never lose track of what variety actually sits where.
- When a buyer or a Rainforest Alliance / organic auditor asks me to prove my real productive tree stand and that a 'Geisha lot' is genuinely Geisha down to the row, I want a defensible, geotagged census with photos and history, so I can pass the audit and command a reserve/Geisha premium with evidence.

**Features:**
- **Per-tree census registry (tree as a first-class entity)** — Replaces the single aggregate plots.trees count with real individual trees, each with GPS point, variety, planting date, rootstock, and current status (productive / young / stumped / dead / gap). Turns 'about 14,800 trees' into a defensible, queryable stand you can audit, value, and plan against.
- **GPS / map-walk field tagging (QR + offline mobile)** — Crew leads walk a block with a phone, drop or scan each tree, and capture status/photo offline — syncing later. A printed/engraved QR or RFID tag on flagship Geisha trees makes every future visit a 1-second scan instead of guesswork, and gives reserve lots tree-level provenance.
- **Lifecycle & pruning-cycle engine** — Models each tree's stage (nursery → transplant → year-1/2/3 establishment → full production → light-prune / heavy-prune / stump-and-regenerate → senescence → removal) and the years-since-last-stump. Encodes the real agronomy: trees regenerate ~2 yrs after stumping, peak in early production, decline with age — so the system tells you which trees are due for which cut.
- **Cyclic 'rock-and-roll' stumping planner** — Schedules renovation across rows so only a fraction of a block is in regeneration at once, protecting overall volume. Generates the year's pruning/stumping work and pushes it straight into the existing Tasks module (Pruning/Planting categories) assigned to crews.
- **Gap & stand-count tracker with replant queue** — Finds missing/dead trees (gaps) per block, quantifies the real vs. nominal stand, and builds a prioritized replant list tied to nursery availability — so density stays at target and 'expected yield' is grounded in trees that actually exist.
- **Nursery-to-field pipeline** — Tracks seedling batches from germination through hardening to field transplant, then links each transplanted seedling to its tree record and GPS spot — closing the loop between propagation, replanting, and the census.
- **Tree-age & productive-life yield curve / renovation-ROI view** — Overlays block age structure on the arabica productive-life curve and projects the yield (and revenue) consequence of renovating now vs. later — turning stumping/replant decisions into a capital plan the owner can act on, with a forecast of when renovated trees return to full bearing.
- **Drone/satellite census reconciliation** — Imports an aerial tree-count/crown-segmentation pass and reconciles it against the ground census to catch miscounts, validate gaps at scale, and keep a 60-hectare-plus stand current without walking every row every season.

**Integrations / vendors / hardware:** Esri ArcGIS Field Maps and/or QField + QGIS — offline GPS field collection of geotagged tree points/polygons, the de-facto open + enterprise standard for field inventory, RTK / high-precision GNSS (e.g. Trimble Catalyst, Emlid Reach, or DJI RTK) — sub-meter to centimeter tree-point accuracy so trees as close as ~0.5 m apart resolve individually, Pix4Dfields / DroneDeploy + DJI (Phantom 4 RTK / Mavic 3 Multispectral) — drone orthomosaics feeding deep-learning tree-crown detection/counting (Mask R-CNN / YOLO-class models) for stand reconciliation, RFID / NFC and QR tagging (TreeO2-style approach) — durable per-tree tags for instant rescans of flagship Geisha/reserve trees, Sentinel-2 / Planet NDVI tiles — block-level vigor context layered over the census to flag declining sections worth a renovation look, Cropster (downstream) — passing tree/lot provenance through to roast/cupping records so a reserve lot's tree-level story survives into the cup, Certification bodies — Rainforest Alliance / SCA / organic (USDA/EU) auditors consume the geotagged stand + history as evidence, GS1 / lot-traceability standards and EUDR geolocation requirements — geotagged plots/trees satisfy the EU Deforestation Regulation plot-polygon mandate for exported green coffee

**Data model additions:** trees — first-class per-tree entity: id, plot_id (FK), variety, geo_lat/geo_lng (or PostGIS point), planted_date, rootstock, row/position, status (nursery|young|productive|stumped|declining|dead|gap), tag_code (QR/RFID), photo_url, current_health, source_seedling_id, tree_lifecycle_events — append-only history per tree: tree_id, event_type (planted|transplanted|light_prune|heavy_prune|stumped|fertilized|recorded_dead|removed|replanted), event_date, worker_id, notes, next_due_date — drives the pruning-cycle engine and audit trail, tree_census_surveys — a dated block-walk/survey pass: id, plot_id, surveyed_by, started_at, completed_at, method (ground|drone|reconciled), counts (productive/gap/dead) snapshot, nursery_batches — seedling propagation: id, variety, sown_date, source (seed_lot/cutting), quantity, stage (germination|hardening|ready), expected_transplant_date, replant_queue — prioritized gaps awaiting replant: plot_id, gap_count, target_variety, nursery_batch_id, scheduled_date (feeds Tasks), plot age/stand rollups — derived: real stand count, gap %, age distribution, % in regeneration — replacing the static plots.trees and grounding plots.expected_yield_kg

**AI / automation:**
- Drone-imagery tree detection & counting (Mask R-CNN / YOLO crown segmentation) to auto-census a block and flag gaps and dead trees at scale instead of manual row-walks
- On-device leaf/branch photo diagnosis at scan time — rust (roya), CBB/broca, nutrient deficiency detection from a phone photo, attached to the tree record (mobile CNN models with GPS-tagged reporting already proven for coffee leaf disease)
- Renovation-timing recommender: given each tree's age, stumping history, and recent per-tree/per-block yield, predict the optimal stump/replant year and auto-draft the cyclic rock-and-roll schedule to maximize multi-year volume
- Yield forecasting from stand structure: combine tree age distribution, the arabica productive-life curve, and weather to forecast block/farm volume and the volume dip from any proposed renovation
- Anomaly/gap detection reconciling ground census vs. drone count vs. nominal density, surfacing 'you think you have 14,800 trees here, the stand is really 13,210 with 9% gaps' as an alert

**World-class:** "World-class is a living digital twin of every productive tree on the finca: open the map, tap any dot, and see that tree's variety, exact age, full prune/stump history, last three seasons of yield, latest health photo, and its scheduled next cut — all captured offline on a phone walk and reconciled against a drone pass each season. The pruning-cycle engine runs the farm on a deliberate rock-and-roll renovation rhythm (a planned slice stumped each year, never the whole block) so volume never collapses, and every replant flows from the nursery into the exact GPS gap it fills. The stand count feeding 'expected yield' is real, not a round number, and the entire geotagged census is export-ready for EUDR plot-polygon compliance, Rainforest Alliance/organic audits, and reserve-lot provenance — proving a Geisha block is Geisha tree-by-tree."

**Coffee-specific angle:** "Janson grows Geisha — the most valuable arabica on earth — alongside Caturra, Catuaí, Pacamara and Typica across 1,350–1,700 masl. Geisha trees command auction-level premiums, so individual high-value Geisha and reserve trees genuinely merit per-tree tagging and provenance the way no commodity crop would; tree-level traceability turns 'a Geisha lot' into a defensible, premium-justifying story. The varietal mix also means each plot has a different productive-life curve and pruning response (Pacamara and Geisha behave differently from Caturra), so a per-tree lifecycle model lets Janson renovate the right varietal blocks on the right cycle. Volcán's elevation, the 200-ha quetzal reserve, and the agroforestry/shade canopy make this a long-horizon, conservation-minded estate where deliberate, tree-by-tree renovation (not clear-and-replant) fits both the brand and EUDR/Rainforest Alliance expectations Janson must meet to export specialty green to premium buyers."


#### Shade, agroforestry & biodiversity — managing the 200ha reserve
*Priority:* **differentiator**  ·  *Opportunity:* **9/10**

**Persona:** Primary: the farm agronomist / conservation steward (at Janson, the family member or staff agronomist who owns both coffee production AND the 200ha private reserve). Secondary: the certification & compliance lead (handles Bird Friendly / Rainforest Alliance / EUDR audits), and the brand/tourism owner who turns the quetzal-habitat story into selling power and reserve eco-tourism. Tertiary stakeholder: the auditor/verifier who needs trustworthy evidence on demand.

**Jobs-to-be-done:**
- When I plan next season's plantings and prunings, I want to know each plot's actual canopy shade %, tree-species count, and native ratio against the Smithsonian Bird Friendly thresholds (40% shade, 10 species/ha, native majority), so I can keep certification without guessing and avoid over- or under-shading the Geisha.
- When a quetzal is reported on the reserve or I hear one of our AudioMoth recorders flag it, I want every detection logged to a place and date with the surrounding aguacatillo (Ocotea) fruiting trees mapped, so I can prove the reserve is functioning quetzal habitat and protect the trees the birds depend on.
- When an EU buyer or Bird Friendly auditor asks me to prove no deforestation happened on a plot since 2020, I want to produce the plot polygon, the dated satellite/NDVI history, and the shade-tree inventory in one export, so I pass the audit in an afternoon instead of scrambling for weeks.
- When I'm deciding whether to enroll the reserve in a carbon or biodiversity-credit program, I want a running estimate of standing biomass, canopy cover trend, and the species we host, so I can quantify the reserve's value beyond coffee and open a new revenue line.

**Features:**
- **Agroforestry Canopy & Shade-Tree Inventory** — A per-plot register of shade/companion trees (species, native vs exotic, DBH, height, canopy diameter, planting year, function: nitrogen-fixer/timber/fruit/quetzal-food) that auto-computes shade %, species-per-hectare, and native ratio and scores each plot against Bird Friendly / Rainforest Alliance thresholds. Outcome: certification compliance becomes a live dashboard, not an annual fire drill, and shade decisions are data-driven.
- **Reserve Biodiversity & Species Observation Log** — A geolocated observation ledger for the 200ha reserve (birds incl. quetzal, mammals, amphibians, indicator insects) with photo/audio attachments, observer, GPS point, and IUCN status, plus a species richness trend over time. Outcome: turns the reserve from an unmeasured asset into a quantified, defensible biodiversity story for buyers, certifiers, and credit programs.
- **Bioacoustic Monitoring Ingest (AudioMoth + BirdNET)** — Ingests passive-acoustic recorder detections (AudioMoth units running BirdNET/Wildlife Acoustics Kaleidoscope classification) into the species log automatically, with confidence scores and recorder-station placement on the map. Outcome: continuous, low-labor proof of bird activity — especially cryptic species and the resplendent quetzal — without a full-time ornithologist.
- **Satellite Canopy & Deforestation Monitor (NDVI / forest-change)** — Pulls periodic NDVI and forest-change tiles for plot and reserve polygons, flags any canopy loss against a December-2020 baseline, and stores the dated history. Outcome: continuous EUDR-grade deforestation evidence and early detection of canopy decline or illegal clearing on the reserve edge.
- **Plot & Reserve Polygon / Geo-boundary Manager** — Captures and stores GPS polygons for every coffee plot and reserve management zone (core forest, buffer, riparian corridor, restoration area), the EUDR-required geolocation artifact. Outcome: the single geospatial backbone that makes EUDR statements, shade math, and species mapping all spatially exact.
- **Certification & EUDR Compliance Cockpit** — Maps live farm data to each scheme's criteria (Smithsonian Bird Friendly, Rainforest Alliance, EUDR due-diligence) with a red/amber/green readiness panel, gap list, document vault, and one-click audit/export pack. Outcome: certifications are renewed faster and at lower risk; EUDR due-diligence statements generate from the data already in the system.
- **Carbon & Biomass / Ecosystem-Services Estimator** — Estimates standing above-ground biomass and CO2 from the tree inventory using allometric equations, tracks canopy-cover trend, and packages it for carbon/biodiversity credit registries (Verra, Plan Vivo). Outcome: surfaces a potential non-coffee revenue line and lets the family value the reserve as a carbon and biodiversity asset.
- **Restoration & Native-Tree Planting Planner** — Plans and tracks native enrichment plantings (e.g. Ocotea/aguacatillo for quetzals, Inga for nitrogen-fixing shade), links each planting to a task and crew, and projects when new canopy will close gaps flagged by the shade scorer. Outcome: closes shade and native-ratio gaps deliberately and ties reforestation work into the existing task/worker system.

**Integrations / vendors / hardware:** Smithsonian Bird Friendly certification standard (40% min shade, 10 tree species/ha, native majority, organic, no deforestation in 10 yrs) — encode as compliance rules, Rainforest Alliance / SAN certification criteria and EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) geolocation-polygon + due-diligence-statement format, AudioMoth open-acoustic recorders + BirdNET (Cornell) and Wildlife Acoustics Kaleidoscope for bioacoustic species classification, eBird / Cornell Lab + iNaturalist + GBIF for species reference, observation sync, and IUCN Red List status lookups, Satellite/NDVI and forest-change providers — Sentinel-2 via Sentinel Hub / Copernicus, Planet Labs, and Global Forest Watch (Hansen forest-change) for deforestation baselines, Esri ArcGIS / Mapbox / Leaflet + OpenStreetMap for polygon capture, mapping, and the geospatial layer; GeoJSON as the interchange standard, Carbon & biodiversity credit registries: Verra (VCS), Plan Vivo, Gold Standard; allometric biomass models (e.g. Chave et al.) for AGB/CO2 estimation, Field-data capture tools (KoBoToolbox / ODK / Survey123) and GPS units for offline tree-inventory and observation entry in the reserve, Sociedad Audubon de Panamá / local NGOs and STRI (Smithsonian Tropical Research Institute, Panama) as data and verification partners for the quetzal-habitat narrative

**Data model additions:** shade_trees — id, plot_id (FK plots) or reserve_zone_id, species_scientific, species_common, is_native (bool), function (nitrogen_fixer|timber|fruit|quetzal_food|windbreak), dbh_cm, height_m, canopy_diameter_m, planted_year, health, geo_point, tree_species — scientific_name PK, common_name, family, native_to_chiriqui (bool), is_quetzal_food (bool), iucn_status, growth_form, reserve_zones — id, name, zone_type (core_forest|buffer|riparian_corridor|restoration|coffee), area_ha, polygon (GeoJSON/geometry), management_objective, plot_geometries — plot_id (FK plots), polygon (GeoJSON), centroid, captured_at, source (gps|survey) — EUDR geolocation artifact, species_observations — id, observed_at, reserve_zone_id|plot_id, geo_point, taxon (FK tree_species or a wildlife_taxa table), kind (bird|mammal|amphibian|insect|plant), count, iucn_status, observer, media_url, source (human|bioacoustic|camera_trap), acoustic_detections — id, recorder_station_id, detected_at, taxon, confidence_pct, classifier (BirdNET|Kaleidoscope), audio_clip_url, recorder_stations — id, reserve_zone_id, geo_point, device_model (AudioMoth), deployed_at, status, battery_pct, canopy_measurements — id, plot_id|reserve_zone_id, measured_at, shade_pct, ndvi, canopy_cover_pct, source (satellite|field), provider (Sentinel-2|Planet|manual), deforestation_alerts — id, plot_id|reserve_zone_id, detected_at, baseline_date (2020-12-31), area_lost_ha, severity, provider (GFW|Sentinel), status (open|reviewed|cleared), certifications — id, scheme (bird_friendly|rainforest_alliance|eudr|organic), status, issued_date, expiry_date, criteria_json (per-criterion pass/fail), document_vault_refs, carbon_estimates — id, reserve_zone_id|plot_id, estimated_at, above_ground_biomass_t, co2e_t, method (allometric model ref), tree_count_basis, restoration_plantings — id, reserve_zone_id|plot_id, species (FK tree_species), planted_count, planted_date, task_id (FK tasks), survival_pct, projected_canopy_closure_year

**AI / automation:**
- Bioacoustic species ID (BirdNET/Kaleidoscope) auto-classifying AudioMoth recordings into the species log with confidence scores — continuous bird monitoring without an on-site ornithologist
- Satellite NDVI/forest-change anomaly detection that auto-flags canopy loss against the 2020 EUDR baseline and raises a deforestation_alert with a map and dated before/after tiles
- AI shade advisor: given a plot's current canopy %, native ratio, and species count, recommend which native trees to interplant (favoring quetzal-food Ocotea and N-fixing Inga) and project the canopy-closure date to hit Bird Friendly's 40%
- Photo-based tree/wildlife identification (iNaturalist-style vision model) so field staff can log a tree or bird from a phone photo and auto-fill species, native flag, and IUCN status
- Auto-generated certification & EUDR readiness narrative — the system reads live data, drafts the due-diligence statement, lists gaps, and packages the audit evidence pack
- Canopy-structure estimation from satellite/drone imagery (the Smithsonian–NASA approach) to cross-check field-measured shade % and detect under/over-shading at scale
- Camera-trap image classification (Wildlife Insights-style) to turn reserve camera-trap photos into mammal observations automatically

**World-class:** World-class is a living digital twin of the farm-plus-reserve where every plot and every reserve zone is a polygon carrying its real canopy structure, tree inventory, and species record, continuously refreshed by three independent evidence streams — satellite NDVI/forest-change, passive bioacoustic recorders, and human field observations — that all reconcile against each other. Certification and EUDR readiness is always green-on-arrival: an auditor's request is answered with a one-click, geolocated, time-stamped evidence pack rather than a three-week scramble. The system doesn't just record shade, it advises it: it knows that a given Geisha plot is drifting below 40% shade, recommends the native species to interplant, and projects the canopy-closure date. The 200ha reserve stops being an unmeasured cost center and becomes a quantified asset — biodiversity richness trending up, biomass/CO2 estimated, quetzal detections mapped to the aguacatillo trees they depend on — credible enough to sell to EU buyers, certifiers, eco-tourists, and a carbon registry. The gold standard reference is the Smithsonian Bird Friendly program (now partnered with NASA to remotely monitor shade structure); a world-class Janson system meets that bar with its own continuous data.

**Coffee-specific angle:** This is Janson's single sharpest moat. Almost no farm software treats the reserve as a first-class object — but Janson literally runs a 200ha private nature reserve that is documented quetzal habitat, and grows premium Geisha that commands its price partly on origin and ecological story. The resplendent quetzal's survival in Chiriquí cloud forest is tied to fruiting wild-avocado trees — aguacatillo / Ocotea — so a Janson-specific shade-tree inventory should explicitly tag quetzal-food species and protect/expand them, turning a conservation duty into a marketing and certification asset. Geisha is shade-sensitive and grown at 1,350–1,700 masl in cloud-forest conditions, so canopy management is directly a cup-quality lever, not just compliance. And because the EUDR is now live for coffee (large operators Dec 2025, SMEs June 2026), a Volcán exporter selling green coffee to Europe MUST produce plot polygons and post-2020 no-deforestation proof — this capability turns a regulatory threat into a turnkey export advantage, and lets Janson credibly pursue Smithsonian Bird Friendly status, the gold standard for shade-grown coffee.


#### Nursery & seedling propagation management
*Priority:* **differentiator**  ·  *Opportunity:* **8/10**

**Persona:** Janette, the farm Agronomist / Nursery Manager (the "w-02 Agronomist" already in the data model). Secondary users: Don Janson / farm owner (renovation & variety-portfolio planning and ROI), the Supervisor (Miguel, w-01) who schedules the labor crews that pot, prick-out and transplant, and the field crew leads who execute transplanting to specific plots/blocks.

**Jobs-to-be-done:**
- When I receive a high-value lot of Geisha seed from a trusted mother garden or a World Coffee Research verified source, I want to register its genetic identity, source, viability test and germination batch in one place, so I can prove provenance years later and never plant out a mislabeled or low-vigor variety into a $/lb-premium block.
- When I am running 30,000+ seedlings across germination beds and shade-house bags through a 6-12 month cycle at 1,400-1,700 masl, I want to track each batch's stage, count, germination/survival rate and the misting/shade/fertigation it received, so I can predict exactly how many transplant-ready, true-to-type plants I'll have for each block's renovation and not over- or under-sow.
- When frost, broca or a drought stress event hits the shade house or a block needs re-establishment after stumping, I want to know my live transplant-ready inventory by variety, age and hardiness, so I can fill the gap from my own nursery instead of buying unknown-provenance plants at the worst possible moment.
- When a crew transplants a seedling batch into Tizingal Alto or Río Sereno, I want the seedling's full lineage (seed lot -> germination batch -> nursery bench -> field tree) to carry forward into the plot and eventual harvest lot, so a cup-quality or yield result can be traced all the way back to its genetics and nursery treatment.

**Features:**
- **Seed Lot & Genetic Provenance Registry** — Every incoming seed/cutting lot is recorded with variety, source (own mother tree, neighbor, WCR verified seed lot, importer), lot weight, purchase cost, viability/germination test result, and an optional genetic-verification reference. Gives Janson defensible, audit-ready provenance for premium Geisha and an honest variety story for buyers.
- **Germination & Seedling Batch Lifecycle Tracker** — A batch moves through soaking -> germination bed (matchstick/soldadito) -> pricked-out into bags -> shade-house grow-out -> hardening-off -> transplant-ready, each stage time-stamped with live plant counts so germination % and survival % are computed automatically. Turns an opaque 6-12 month pipeline into a measurable, forecastable inventory.
- **Live Nursery Inventory & Transplant-Readiness Board** — Real-time count of plants by variety, age, stage and bench/bed location, with a readiness flag (height, leaf-pairs, hardened). Lets the owner and agronomist see at a glance how many true-to-type plants are available to renovate each block, and feeds a shortfall/surplus alert.
- **Renovation & Sowing Planner (nursery -> plot)** — Plan backward from each plot's renovation target (replace frost-damaged Río Sereno rows, fill density gaps): given trees needed, target plant-out date, expected germination + survival rates and the 6-12 month altitude cycle, it tells the agronomist exactly how many seeds of which variety to sow and when. Closes the loop with the existing 'Planting' tasks and plots.establishedYear.
- **Nursery Environment & Care Log** — Logs misting/irrigation, shade-cloth %, fertigation, fungicide/pest scouting (damping-off, leaf miner, rust on seedlings) per bench, optionally fed by IoT soil-moisture/temperature/light sensors. Ties seedling vigor and survival back to the care it received so the nursery protocol improves season over season.
- **Seedling -> Field Traceability Link** — On transplant, a batch is bound to a plot (and ultimately the trees/harvest lot codes the system already uses), so lineage flows seed lot -> germination batch -> field block -> harvest/processing lot. Makes a cup score or yield outcome traceable to genetics and nursery treatment, and underpins certification and the Janson origin story.

**Integrations / vendors / hardware:** World Coffee Research — Verified Seed / S&H (Seed & Healthy plant) program and the WCR Coffee Variety catalog (variety identity, genetic-purity reference, nursery best-practice manuals), Cropster Cultivate / Cropster Origin (linking nursery batch + farm lot lineage into the processing and cupping traceability the farm already aims for), IoT sensing for shade-house environment: Sensoterra / METER Group TEROS soil-moisture probes, SensorPush or Pessl Instruments (Metos) temperature/humidity/light dataloggers, with LoRaWAN backhaul for low-cost rural coverage, QR/RFID labeling — DYMO / Brother / Zebra label printers and standard QR encoding for per-bench / per-batch tags scannable from the existing mobile UI, Panama MIDA (Ministerio de Desarrollo Agropecuario) and ISA / phytosanitary nursery registration and any import permits for seed brought across the Costa Rica border at Río Sereno, GLOBALG.A.P. / Rainforest Alliance / organic certification record hooks (planting material provenance and agro-chemical-use logs are audited line items), Open-Meteo / NOAA / Pessl weather feeds (already used by the app's weather strip) to drive frost and heat-stress alerts on the shade house at altitude

**Data model additions:** seed_lots (id, variety, source_type [own-mother | neighbor | wcr-verified | importer], source_name, received_date, weight_g or seed_count, cost_usd, viability_pct, genetic_ref, notes), mother_trees (id, plot_id FK, variety, selection_label, established_year) — own-farm seed source provenance, ties into existing plots, seedling_batches (id, seed_lot_id FK, variety, sown_date, sown_count, current_count, stage [soaking | germination-bed | pricked-out | grow-out | hardening | transplant-ready | planted-out | culled], germination_pct, survival_pct, expected_ready_date, bench_id FK), nursery_benches / beds (id, type [germination-bed | shade-house-bench], capacity, shade_pct, location, sensor_id nullable), nursery_care_events (id, batch_id FK or bench_id FK, date, type [misting | fertigation | fungicide | scouting | shade-adjust], product, dose, observation, performed_by worker_id FK), transplant_events (id, batch_id FK, plot_id FK, date, plant_count, performed_by worker_id FK) — the seedling->field lineage link, and the natural place to stamp the future tree/harvest-lot ancestry, new enum nursery_stage; extend task_category usage so existing 'Planting' tasks can reference a seedling_batch_id

**AI / automation:**
- Per-variety, per-altitude germination & survival forecasting: learn the farm's own historical curves to predict transplant-ready plant counts and dates within a few percent, replacing rule-of-thumb over-sowing.
- Auto sow-quantity & timing recommendation: from a block's renovation target, target plant-out date and the forecast curves, compute exactly how many seeds of which variety to sow and when (back-planning the 6-12 month cycle).
- Computer-vision seedling grading from a phone photo of a bench: estimate stand count, height/leaf-pair stage, and flag damping-off / leaf-miner / rust so counts and readiness update without hand-counting thousands of bags.
- Shade-house early-warning: fuse IoT soil-moisture/temperature/light with the weather feed to predict frost or heat/drought stress and prescribe misting, shade-cloth or fertigation actions before damage, auto-raising a Pest Control / Irrigation task to the right worker.
- Off-type / purity anomaly flag: surface batches whose growth or morphology deviates from the variety's expected pattern as candidate genetic off-types to rogue out before they reach a premium block.

**World-class:** A world-class coffee-nursery module makes a 6-12 month, multi-thousand-plant biological pipeline as legible and forecastable as a factory line. It guarantees genetic purity and provenance from a WCR-verified or known mother-tree seed lot all the way to the field tree and the cup, with one scan. It forecasts transplant-ready inventory by variety and date within a few percent by learning the farm's own germination and survival curves at its specific altitudes, and it auto-generates the exact sow quantity and timing to hit each block's renovation plan with zero waste. It catches damping-off, drought or frost stress in the shade house days early via sensor + weather signals and prescribes the corrective action, and it turns the whole nursery into a defensible asset: every premium Geisha lot can prove where its genetics came from and how it was raised. Nothing leaves the nursery untraceable; nothing is planted out unverified.

**Coffee-specific angle:** Janson's economics live and die on Geisha — a variety that is high-value, slow to germinate, genetically sensitive to off-types, and worth protecting provenance on. At 1,350-1,700 masl the nursery cycle stretches toward 12 months, so a sowing mistake costs a full year and an entire renovation window; getting sow-quantity and timing right is uniquely high-stakes here. The farm propagates five varieties (Geisha, Caturra, Catuaí, Pacamara, Typica — already enumerated in the schema), each with different germination and grow-out behavior, so per-variety curves matter. Being adjacent to a 200-hectare private reserve and brand-built on origin integrity, a provenance-and-purity nursery record is also a marketing and certification asset, not just an ops tool — it lets Janson truthfully tell buyers exactly which mother garden and seed lot a given micro-lot's trees descend from. It also closes loops the current app already half-implements: the 'Transplant Geisha seedlings from the nursery' and 'Stump and re-establish frost-damaged rows' Planting tasks, and plots.establishedYear, all point at a nursery the data model does not yet represent.


#### Regenerative & soil-carbon practice tracking
*Priority:* **differentiator**  ·  *Opportunity:* **8/10**

**Persona:** Primary: the farm Agronomist / Sustainability Lead at Janson (the person who already does plot inspections and owns the "Soil" task category today). Secondary stakeholders who consume the same data: the farm owner (Janson family) who funds carbon/biodiversity programs and signs the supply contracts, and the external Rainforest Alliance / Verra verifier-auditor who needs evidence on demand. On a small farm the agronomist is the one who walks the rows, decides on compost vs. biochar vs. cover crop, schedules pruning of shade trees, and is currently the bottleneck because all of this lives in his head and a paper notebook.

**Jobs-to-be-done:**
- When I adopt a regenerative practice on a plot (apply compost, plant a cover crop, add a shade tree, make biochar from coffee husk), I want to log it once with date, plot, amount, and a geotagged photo, so I can prove to a Verra/Rainforest Alliance auditor exactly what we did and when without reconstructing a year from memory.
- When the harvest season ends and I'm deciding where to invest scarce labor and cash next year, I want to see soil organic carbon, shade canopy %, and biodiversity trending per plot against our baseline, so I can target the plots that are degrading and prove the regenerative ones are actually improving (not just claimed).
- When a green-coffee buyer or the EU asks whether our coffee is deforestation-free and regeneratively grown, I want a one-click farm dossier with plot polygons, practice history, and a soil-carbon estimate, so I can close the sale at a premium and stay EUDR-compliant past the Dec 2026 deadline.
- When I take a soil sample and send it to the lab, I want the results to land back against the right plot and depth automatically, so my soil-carbon baseline and re-measurements form a credible time series instead of loose PDFs in an inbox.

**Features:**
- **Practice Log (regenerative event ledger)** — A single append-only timeline of every regenerative action per plot — compost/vermicompost application, biochar (from on-farm coffee husk/pulp), cover cropping, mulching, reduced/no-till, shade-tree planting, living fences, terracing. Each event captures date, plot, practice type, quantity (kg/ha or trees), input source, geotagged photo, and the worker who did it. Turns the existing free-text 'Soil' task into structured, audit-ready evidence — the spine the whole pillar hangs on.
- **Soil Carbon & Health Baseline tracker** — Per-plot, per-depth soil sampling records (SOC %, bulk density, pH, N-P-K, CEC, organic matter) with a baseline year and re-measurement series, auto-charting SOC stock (tonnes C/ha) over time. Gives the agronomist a defensible time series and the farm a quantified carbon asset rather than a vague 'we're regenerative' claim.
- **Shade Canopy & Agroforestry register** — Extends each plot beyond today's single shadePct number into a canopy model: shade-tree species mix (Inga, Erythrina, native hardwoods), stems/ha, canopy cover trend from satellite, and a target shade band per altitude/variety. Quetzal-friendly multi-strata shade is Janson's signature — this makes it measurable and managed, not anecdotal.
- **Carbon & Practice MRV dossier export** — Generates the evidence package a Verra VM0042 / Rainforest Alliance Regenerative auditor or a green-coffee buyer actually needs: plot polygons, practice history, soil-carbon series, shade trend, with versioned snapshots. Converts diligent record-keeping directly into carbon-credit revenue, certification, and price premiums.
- **Biodiversity & reserve monitoring** — Logs the 200-ha private reserve as first-class farm asset: bird/species sightings (quetzal flagship), riparian buffers, water-source protection, restored hectares. Buyers and certifiers increasingly pay for biodiversity outcomes, not just carbon — and it's the part of Janson's story that no competitor can copy.
- **EUDR geolocation & deforestation-free compliance** — Stores legally-required plot boundary polygons (lat/long perimeter for plots >4 ha) and a deforestation-free attestation with a forest-cover-loss check, so Janson can sell into the EU after the 30 Dec 2026 deadline instead of being locked out. Table-stakes for market access, but it shares the exact same plot-geometry and practice data the regenerative features need.

**Integrations / vendors / hardware:** Verra Registry / VM0042 'Improved Agricultural Land Management' v2.2 + ISO 14064-2 (soil-carbon crediting methodology and dMRV reporting format), Rainforest Alliance Regenerative Agriculture Standard (in effect 1 Mar 2026) + their certification/geodata platform for audit and chain-of-custody, Sentinel-2 (ESA Copernicus, free 10 m) and EOSDA / Sentera / Farmonaut agriculture APIs for NDVI, canopy cover, and forest-loss change detection, EU TRACES / EUDR Due Diligence Statement submission + Global Forest Watch / JRC forest-baseline layers for deforestation-free verification, Soil lab integration (e.g. a regional Panamá/Costa Rica agronomy lab, or international labs like Ward/Eurofins) — LIMS PDF/CSV result ingestion against plot+depth, IoT soil probes (Teralytic 26-sensor probe, or low-cost LoRaWAN moisture/temp/EC sensors) for continuous in-situ soil moisture, EC and temperature, Regenerative Organic Certified (ROC) and Gold Standard as alternate certification/credit rails, Drone / multispectral imagery (DJI + open tools) and optional LiDAR for canopy-height and biomass modeling, Cornell Lab Merlin/eBird-style acoustic + sighting feeds for biodiversity (quetzal) monitoring on the reserve

**Data model additions:** practice_events (id, plot_id, practice_type enum[compost|vermicompost|biochar|cover_crop|mulch|no_till|shade_planting|terracing|living_fence], date, quantity, unit, input_source, worker_id, photo_url, notes), soil_samples (id, plot_id, sampled_date, depth_cm, soc_pct, bulk_density, ph, organic_matter_pct, n_pct, p_ppm, k_ppm, cec, lab_ref, is_baseline), soil_carbon_estimates (id, plot_id, as_of_date, soc_stock_t_per_ha, method enum[sampled|modeled|blended], baseline_soc, delta_vs_baseline), shade_trees / canopy_records (plot_id, species, stems_per_ha, canopy_cover_pct, source enum[manual|satellite|drone], measured_date) — extends Plot.shadePct, plot_geometry (plot_id, boundary_polygon geojson, centroid, eudr_compliant bool, deforestation_check_date, forest_baseline_year), biodiversity_observations (id, location_ref, species, count, observed_at, method enum[sighting|acoustic|camera_trap], observer), ndvi_readings (plot_id, captured_date, ndvi_mean, canopy_cover_pct, source) — satellite time series, soil_sensor_readings (sensor_id, plot_id, ts, moisture_pct, temp_c, ec, depth_cm) — IoT stream, certifications / mrv_snapshots (id, scheme enum[verra|rainforest_alliance|roc], status, period, evidence_bundle_url, verified_by, version)

**AI / automation:**
- Auto-estimate SOC stock per plot between physical samples by blending sparse lab samples with Sentinel-2 NDVI, slope/altitude and logged practices (the Verra dMRV 'sampling + biogeochemical model' pattern) — turn 2 soil tests a year into a continuous, defensible carbon curve.
- Photo-to-practice classification: snap a geotagged photo of a compost pile, cover crop, or new shade seedling and have the model pre-fill the practice_event type, plot, and rough quantity, so logging in the field is one tap.
